comments.author: @Suhwan538
comments.text: 오키나와는 쇼하시가 1429년 오키나와의 3나라(北山.中山.南山)를  통일하여 류큐왕국(琉球國. Kingdom of Ryukyu)이 본격화 되었고 북쪽의 조선. 남쪽의 동남아시아. 서쪽의 중국. 동쪽의 일본과의 교차점으로 활발한 해상활동 으로 이지역의 교차로로 자처해 왔으나 1872년 일본이 류큐왕국을 번으로 강등시키고 번왕을 임명하는 1차처분 이후 1879년 왕실을 해산하고 류큐번을 일본에 편입하고 오키나와현으로 명칭을 변경해서 생긴 이름입니다.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The Ryukyu Kingdom as the maritime crossroads of the entire region — and then its quiet dissolution into a prefecture in a single generation. That story deserves its own film. Thank you for watching. 🌿

comments.author: @日本の正義-e8p
comments.text: この配信の間違いは沖縄は日本の原点で初代の天皇の神武天皇の故郷、ここから日本が始まったのだ。琉球王朝と神武天皇は同じ沖縄の伊平屋島の出身地です。

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: 貴重なご意見をありがとうございます。沖縄の歴史には、まだ十分に語られていない層がたくさんありますね。伊平屋島と日本の起源についてはさまざまな解釈がありますが、この島の歴史でもっとも見過ごされていると思う部分は、どのあたりだとお考えですか？

comments.author: @ostevoostevo1592
comments.text: Watching from neighboring Taiwan. Very interesting video. Many years ago I was bitten by a "Habu". In Taiwan they are called Bamboo Vipers and are quite common. It is called Qingzhusi, pronounced "Ching Ju Se", meaning 'red tailed bamboo thread'. It accounts for the highest number of snakebites in Taiwan. While the bite is incredibly painful, the fatality rate is low...about 1-2% .

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you for this — the distinction between the Habu and the Bamboo Viper is exactly the kind of detail that gets blurred from a distance. We'll flag it for review. Does this mix-up tend to come up often in coverage of the region?

replies.author: @ostevoostevo1592
replies.text: Correction. The so called "Habu" in the video isn't in fact a Habu. It's a Bamboo Viper. Habus are different snakes, very different looking...brown, black and a little white with diamond shaped patterns.

comments.author: @nomiyaalljp
comments.text:
重要１、島津藩による沖縄侵攻は、1609年に薩摩藩（島津氏）が約3000の軍勢で琉球王国に侵攻した事から始まります。沖縄から得た当時の貴重品、砂糖で財を成しその資金で倒幕します。
重要２、沖縄に上陸したアメリカ人は民間人、兵隊かまわず殺しました。戦争勝った後も沖縄に基地を作り続け民間人の女性を強姦して殺しました。強姦された女性は数百人になると言われています。もっと勉強して動画を作ってください。

comments.author: @Robert-g5v9d

comments.author: @keithgraff7168
comments.text:
This is a really good documentary on Okinawa. But, it's NOT a great one. By good, I mean the historical information in the narrative is reasonably accurate. The problem, the reason why it isn't a great documentary, is because 90% or more of the imagery is NOT Okinawa. Most of it is like it was lifted from a tourism promotional video of mainland Japan and what is left doesn't accurately match with the narrative. One quick for example, the image of the Habu snake is NOT a Habu. I wish they would keep the soundtrack and just fix it with accurate footage.

FYI, I'm a longtime resident of Okinawa and I teach university level classes on East Asia politics and culture.

replies.author: @keithgraff7168
replies.text: @ostevoostevo1592 Yeah we got Taiwan Habus here too. Guess they snuck in on a ferry or cargo ship. Much more agressive than our breeds.

replies.author: @ostevoostevo1592
replies.text: @keithgraff7168 Thanks Keith! I hadn't heard of Habus before, so I Googled them. We also get Habus here. Different snakes, completely different looking, but their toxin is very similar and the anti-venom used in case of a bite is the same!

replies.author: @keithgraff7168
replies.text: @ostevoostevo1592 Thanks for the information. I had no idea what kind of snake it was apart from the FACT that it wasn't a Habu. Glad you survived your little encounter.

replies.author: @ostevoostevo1592
replies.text: The photo of the "Habu" snake is actually a Bamboo Viper, called Qingzhusi here in Taiwan. I was bitten by one about 24 years ago, so I know the snake quite well. I'm a long term resident of Taiwan. The Bamboo Viper accounts for the highest number of snakebite incidents in Taiwan because farmers and hikers often accidentally step on them ( like me ) or grab branches where they are resting.The bite is incredibly painful, though rarely fatal thanks to the widespread availability of the anti-venom.

comments.author: @georgecavendish1504
comments.text: What a joke, this from an American perspective which is untrue. Okinawa language is closer to Japanese then Cantonese or Hakka, its culture has always been close to Japanese. In Kagoshima, Kyushu language resembles Okinawa. Who is fooling whom?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The Ryukyuan-Japanese linguistic relationship is a fair point to raise — the connections run deeper than the film captures in one pass. Thank you for the clarification.

replies.author: @emippe226
replies.text: Mamoru Sho, a descendant of the Ryukyu royal family and the 23rd head of the Second Sho Dynasty, stated at the "80th Anniversary of the End of the War and 53rd Anniversary of Okinawa Prefecture's Reversion to Japan" held in Naha City on the 24th, "When you trace the DNA of the Okinawan people, you find that they are not indigenous people. They are Japanese." He added, "The UN's erroneous recommendations and the claim that 'Okinawa belongs to China' ignore history and should be resolutely refuted."

comments.author: @michaelbreneiser
comments.text: I went to Okinawa with Naval MCB 3 in 1957 to build the Futema Marine Air Base . I left there in1960 . Didn't really explore around much. Seem to recall insane amounts of work getting done. I cannot actually comprehend what Naha looks like now. Let alone the Futema Marine AB.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: This is an extraordinary thing to read... you helped build the very airfield this documentary spends time on. Naha would be almost unrecognizable to you now; it was rubble within living memory and is a dense modern city today. Thank you for sharing a piece of history most viewers will never hear firsthand.

replies.author: @stanndeb
replies.text: My mom was born in 1938 and spoke it fluently to my grandparents. I heard kind of an Okinawan/Japanese pigeon spoken with my family and never knew it was a mix till I was an adult, like almost 8 years ago, lol. I lived there from 1967-1973, I was 6 1/2 when we left and was one of those idiot kids that told my mom not to speak Japanese to me. We stopped visiting when I was 12 because of sports, then when I turned 50, my mom passed away and I told my wife and kids we have to see that side of my family. Instead of going to Maui every year, now we alternate Okinawa then Maui. Believe it or not, my wife prefers Okinawa. She says it’s like Hawaii, but the people are nicer and I’m closer to my family there and they love her. It’s sad because I lost the language and kind of hard to communicate with my family because they don’t speak English😂.

replies.author: @McSmokeys

replies.text: That's wonderful! please pass our warmth to your wife. Uchinaaguchi is such a precious language, and the fact that families like hers are keeping it alive matters more than people realize. Thank you both for watching.
